description: Pre-Phase-3 generation baseline. Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct (NF4) running through the production retrieval stack (hybrid + cross-encoder rerank, recursive chunking, top_k=5, max_context_tokens=3500). Judged by Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (NF4, temperature=0). Faithfulness uses RAGAS-style claim-level decomposition with per-chunk attribution: extract atomic claims from the answer, then for each claim identify which retrieved chunk supports it (or 'none'). Score is n_supported_claims / n_total_claims, continuous in [0, 1]. Relevance still uses the 5-anchor absolute scale. Citation grounding and refusal behavior measured by deterministic rule-based checks. This is the starting line Phase 3 fine-tuning will be measured against.

captured_from.notes: Schema version bumped 1 → 2 with the 2026-05-06 methodology change from absolute-scale faithfulness to claim-level decomposition. The earlier absolute-scale numbers (faithfulness mean 0.750/0.758 across the eval sets, with anchor saturation at 0.75) are preserved in git history; the v1 schema reflected those numbers. The no-answer report was re-run after a refusal-pattern coverage gap was discovered and patched (commit 87da219). The curated re-run after the methodology change also surfaced a max_new_tokens truncation bug in the attribution call that was fixed (commit 09a24cc) before the canonical run. See evaluations/analyses/2026-05-06-baseline-generation-eval.md for the full methodology iteration.

no_answer.note: Original measurement reported 0.375; the discrepancy was a refusal-pattern coverage gap in check_no_answer_behavior, not a model behavior issue. All 8 queries produced a refusal opening 'I don't have enough context to answer that.' but only 3 had a follow-up sentence triggering the existing context_doesnt pattern. Pattern set expanded with dont_have_context (commit 87da219) and re-run produced this 1.000 figure.

timing_ms_per_query._schema_note: AWS-standard latency reporting: p50 (typical UX), p95 (slow tail without single-outlier dominance), p99 (1-in-100 tail). p90 and max were dropped — p95 supersedes p90, and max is recoverable from per_query data when needed and is too sensitive to one-off slow events (cold-start warmup, GC pause) to be a useful summary at small N. Mean is included alongside the percentiles because mean ≫ p50 reveals heavy-tailed distributions.

curated.note: retrieve p99=8714 ms reflects a one-time cross-encoder warmup on the first query. Latency unchanged from prior measurement — methodology change is in the judging path only, which doesn't enter retrieve/generate timing.

no_answer.note: Generation is much faster on no-answer queries (~2.7 s p50 vs ~10-15 s in-corpus) because refusal answers are short. retrieve_ms p95/p99 are dominated by a single 9.6 s cross-encoder cold-start outlier; n=8 is too small for stable tail estimates.

headline_findings: Faithfulness mean is 0.853 on both eval sets — exact cross-set agreement validates the methodology. The score distribution is meaningful (curated: 13×1.0, 4×0.75-1.0, 1×0.5-0.75, 2×0.0; structural: 21×1.0, 6×0.75-1.0, 1×0.5-0.75, 1×0.25-0.5, 1×0.0). Median is 1.0 on both sets, mean drops to 0.853 because of the 0.0/low outliers — right-skewed distribution typical of well-grounded answers with occasional misses.

Cross-claim support rate: 0.89 curated (114/128) and 0.94 structural (146/156). Curated is lower largely because of one parser-broken query (curated_001 has 8 claims all PARSE_FAILED, dragging support rate down by 8/128 = 6.25%). The 'true' support rate ignoring parser issues is ~0.95 on both sets.

Methodology change documented: claim-level decomposition replaced absolute-scale anchor scoring (which the prior baseline showed saturating at 0.75 across 49 of 50 in-corpus answers). Two LLM calls per query (extract + attribute) instead of one absolute score. Per-chunk attribution forces evidence commitment and produces actionable per-claim debugging data.

Relevance unchanged in methodology — still 5-anchor absolute. Means 0.725 (curated) / 0.717 (structural), close to prior measurements (0.762 / 0.733). The slight drift is run-to-run judge variance at temperature=0; not a methodological change.

Qwen produces citation markers on ~58% of in-corpus answers (60% curated, 57% structural). Comparable to prior measurement (54%); cross-set agreement is the stable signal. Citation rate is the highest-leverage Phase 3 fine-tune target — gap is large, failure mode is binary (cite-correctly-or-not, no out-of-range hallucinations), and training data is constructible.
Refusal behavior on off-corpus queries is 100% (8/8). Unchanged from prior measurement — no-answer methodology didn't change.

Chunk usage distribution diverges sharply across the two eval sets — chunk 1 is cited LEAST on curated (9 vs avg 26 for 2-5) and MOST on structural (45 vs avg 25 for 2-5). The reranker ordering interacts with query distribution: structural queries (terminology-derived) match chunk 1 directly, while curated queries (paraphrased intent) land grounding evidence in chunks 2-5. Worth a Phase 3 retrieval-side audit — the difference suggests retrieval quality is query-style-dependent in a non-obvious way.

Generation latency: in-corpus p50 ~10-15s, p95 ~20-25s, p99 ~26-30s on RTX 4070 NF4. Off-corpus refusals are much faster (~2.7s p50). Retrieve+rerank latency is sub-second after the cross-encoder warmup on the first query.

Known limitation: claim-level parse failures concentrated on 1 curated query (curated_001, 'How do I install transformers?' — 8/8 claims unparseable) and a few isolated NO_CLAIMS_EXTRACTED cases (1 curated + 2 structural). Both failure modes are flagged in the per_query data with explicit markers (PARSE_FAILED / NO_CLAIMS_EXTRACTED). Total: 5 of 50 in-corpus queries = 10% have at least one parser issue, but only 3 (6%) had their score materially affected (the remaining 47 queries scored cleanly). Worth a small parser-robustness follow-up but doesn't undermine the methodology change.
